Sales techniques can range in all different types of ways. You can get all different types of teachers that can show you many different routes and opportunities to explore when doing lead generation or any kind of sales. I'm going to teach you the technique that teaches you the deep side of selling rather than the wide side of selling. Let me give you a story first of what I mean. Gary Bannerjuck walks into Grant Cardone's office when I'm working there and the entire office, I've never seen this before, because many personalities, large, well-known, not known, rich, poor, and everybody in between is walking to Grant's office to do an interview of some kind. I've never seen the entire office flock to one person who came in. Gary B walks in and the entire office comes around. The first person to walk up to him, this is actually one of the reasons I quit. I go, Gary, I'm thinking about quitting as I'm going to take a selfie. I go, Gary, I'm thinking about quitting and I'm just wondering what I should do. What should I do? He goes, oh, I would quit. You're already thinking about it. I was like, okay, cool, take a selfie. Take a selfie. I'm sweet. That being said, I then watched this man get mobbed by everybody else in the office and I just stood back and watched because I like to observe behavior. I'm into psychology. Grant walks out, of course, and Grant, no, no, a negative way of me saying this has a big ego and it's a good thing. So, Grant walks out and he's like, hey, what's going on, Gary? Nice to see you, man. He shakes his hand, does a cool Grant thing, and Gary shakes his hand as well. It gets to the point where somehow, I think it was Mike Bonnet at the time, asked a question. Like, hey, Gary, what do you like to do for sales training? So, I had a question that was asked about how Gary sells and what his style of selling is. And Gary went on one of his little rants. Now, Gary actually talked to this technique in the context of these specific vocabulary words used to describe what I'm about to say. This technique goes, if you're going far between, I typically refer to it as contextual selling prior to giving this terminology to Gary once again. So, Gary goes, most of you guys in here, it's fantastic that you made 300 calls today, Mike Bonnet. That's great. It's a very wide approach to selling. I personally, I prefer Ann. There was no Ann. He was making some of the episodes. I personally prefer Ann, who's the girl who only makes 40 communications in a day. But those 40 communications are much more in-depth. They have much more data. They have much more reality and context to the conversation of what's being said to the person instead of just that wide approach that you're taking. I want you to know that, in my case, and in my world, Mike, what you just said isn't really going to be a success long-term. You don't have long game with that because you will run out of people at a certain point. However, in my world, when I go deep, I'm going to be able to have more context, more close as a higher closing percentage because you might hit 300 people and close two people. I'm going to hit 40 people and close five. And I was like, whoo! Shit! I understood what happened in that exact moment. Gary came into Grant's office and just put the dog pound down and shut the shit down. All the sales people were sitting there like, that was pretty interesting. You can actually watch a video of it on YouTube. I think it actually is called Gary Shut's Grant Down or something like that. It didn't really happen like that. That wasn't the perception that I'd go at it. But to my perception, I was like, oh wow. Grant doesn't really teach about deep. He teaches about going wide. So this was a new form of selling to me because keep in mind, I was in that environment 13 months at that point. That was the month that I quit. I learned deep that day. And I learned the ability to actually look up somebody, proud of you selling them, to get an idea if they are actually going to be your ideal customer first of all because you're kind of vetting them and qualifying them before you speak. But more importantly, you're creating context for what you're going to say to them. I can't tell you this enough if you really can't get a reality around this right now. That if you reach out to somebody and you have a conversation with them that has context to who they are, what they are, what they're doing, where they're going, instead of just hey, this is so and so with so and so digital agency or you're looking to generate leads to there or something, that's not going to work. And it's not a good game. The depth works. When you reach out to somebody and you say, hey, I see that you're currently doing this on the internet, when I was able to find it looks like you're actually on this is your next play. And I was also able to see kind of what your team looks like right now. I think that I could help you in a few different areas if you can give me five minutes of your time to have a conversation. When you approach somebody with a lot of data on them already, it gives you a different approach and a different perspective in their mindset compared to the person who they can tell is going wide. You see, when you go deep, people also have the ability to sense that intention that you actually research them to figure out who they are, what they might be doing, how you might actually be able to help instead of being another one of their calls on another listing generated. This is an incredible perspective to hold. It really changes your game when you're in a sales market. Comment below if you approach the market with a deep perspective instead of a wide perspective. And if you currently haven't made any sales at all, comment below if you're committed to going deep instead of wide. That way you create more context in the marketplace and good positive rapport and build relationships with people because even if you don't close them, you'll still have more relationships in the end of the day. In the end of the day, the person that has the most relationships and the most support wins.
